title: First-Order Linear ODE

source.file: content/A Practical Guide To Quantitative Finance Interviews Book by Xinfeng Zhou (chapters)/chapter-03-calculus-linear-algebra.md

setup.narrative: Solve the ODE y' + y/x = 1/x^2 with initial condition y(1) = 1, on the interval x > 0.
rules: The equation has the form y' + P(x) y = Q(x), with P(x) = 1/x and Q(x) = 1/x^2.
x > 0 keeps logarithms and divisions clean.
setup.question: What is y(x)?

learningObjectives: Identify the ODE as first-order linear and identify P(x), Q(x).
Compute the integrating factor I(x) = e^(integral P dx).
Recognize that multiplying by I(x) makes the left side d/dx[I(x) * y].

stages.label: Identify the form
stages.prompt: Match y' + y/x = 1/x^2 against y' + P(x) y = Q(x). What are P and Q?
stages.explanation: P is the coefficient of y; Q is the right-hand-side. Here P(x) = 1/x, Q(x) = 1/x^2.

stages.label: Compute the integrating factor
stages.prompt: I(x) = e^(integral P dx) = e^(integral 1/x dx) = ?
stages.explanation: integral 1/x dx = ln(x) (for x > 0; we drop the constant). e^(ln x) = x. So I(x) = x.

stages.label: Multiply through and recognize a perfect derivative
stages.prompt: Multiply the ODE by I(x) = x: x*y' + y = 1/x. The left side simplifies to ?

options.text: (x*y)' -- by the product rule, (x*y)' = x*y' + y.

stages.explanation: The whole point of the integrating factor: the left side x*y' + y is exactly d/dx[x*y] by the product rule. The ODE becomes (x*y)' = 1/x.

stages.label: Integrate both sides

options.text: x*y = ln(x) + c, so y = (ln x + c)/x.

stages.explanation: integral (x*y)' dx = x*y. integral (1/x) dx = ln(x). So x*y = ln(x) + c, hence y = (ln(x) + c)/x.

stages.label: Apply the initial condition
stages.explanation: ln(1) = 0, so y(1) = c. The initial condition c = 1.

stages.label: Reflection
prompts: Why does multiplying by I(x) make the left side a perfect derivative? Where does I'(x) = I(x) * P(x) come from?
What goes wrong if you try the integrating-factor method on a NONLINEAR ODE?
